facts_disclosed: Sister Fatima's GCSE results day — August 21, 2025

turns.content: so my little sister's got her GCSE results coming on August 21 and she's absolutely bricking it. I'm trying to help her revise but I barely have time with work and everything. any tips on how to actually make revision effective when you've only got a few weeks left?

A month out from results day is actually still a solid window to make a real difference — it's more time than most students think they have. What subjects is she most worried about, and how has she been revising so far? That'll help me give more targeted advice.

she's worried about everything tbh but mainly sciences and maths. she's been doing the classic thing of reading through her notes over and over which I KNOW doesn't work but she won't listen to me. she says highlighting helps her remember but it clearly doesn't because she highlighted everything in her biology textbook and still can't explain photosynthesis properly

Ha — the classic "I highlighted everything so now everything is important and therefore nothing is important" approach. Your instinct is right, passive rereading and highlighting are basically the least effective study methods according to pretty much every learning study ever done.

The methods that actually work for GCSEs:

**1. Active recall:** Instead of reading notes, she should close them and try to write down everything she remembers about a topic. The struggle of trying to remember is what builds the memory. Then check what she missed.

**2. Past papers:** This is the single most valuable thing she can do. GCSEs recycle question styles constantly. If she does 5 past papers for each subject, she'll see the patterns. All the exam boards have past papers freely available on their websites.

**3. Spaced repetition:** Instead of studying biology for 6 hours straight, do 45 minutes of biology, then switch to maths, then come back to biology later. The spacing forces her brain to retrieve the information which strengthens it.

What's her name? And what does she want to do after GCSEs — does she have a path in mind?

her name's Fatima. and yeah she wants to do medicine wallah. so she needs proper good grades — like 7s and above ideally. which is why I'm stressing because right now I don't think she's there in the sciences

Medicine is a brilliant goal and it's great she knows what she wants already. But you're right — she'll need strong science GCSEs, especially biology and chemistry, and most med schools want to see 7+ across the board. That's achievable but she needs to switch up her approach now.

For someone aiming for medicine specifically:

**Sciences:** Past papers are absolutely essential. For biology and chemistry, the mark schemes teach you how examiners think. She needs to practice writing answers in the way mark schemes reward — key terms, structured points, not just general understanding. Get her to do a past paper, mark it herself with the mark scheme, and identify exactly where she lost marks.

**Maths:** Practice, practice, practice. There's no shortcut for maths. If she's struggling with specific topics, sites like Corbett Maths and Maths Genie have topic-specific questions with video explanations.

What exam board is she on — AQA, Edexcel, OCR?

AQA I think for most of them. the past papers thing makes sense. I did that when I was revising for my own exams and it worked but she's so stubborn loool she thinks she knows best. I've been trying to sit with her on weekends to go through stuff but I'm shattered by Saturday and she can tell I'm not fully present

You trying to help while exhausted from your own work week is already going above and beyond — she's lucky to have you even if she doesn't realise it right now. But here's the thing: you don't have to be her tutor for every subject. Your job can be more like a study coach — setting up the system and checking in, not doing every session yourself.

Some practical ideas:

**1. Set her up with free resources and let them do the teaching:**
- **Seneca Learning** — free, covers AQA specifically, gamified so it's less painful than reading notes
- **FreeScienceLessons** on YouTube — the best GCSE science channel, covers every AQA topic in short videos
- **Physics & Maths Tutor** — past papers organised by topic with mark schemes

**2. When you do sit with her, test her rather than teach her.** Ask her questions from past papers and get her to explain things to you out loud. If she can explain it, she knows it. If she can't, she knows exactly what to revise next.

**3. Help her make a revision timetable** — even 30 minutes working with her on a plan for the next 4 weeks would be more impactful than exhausted Saturday sessions.

the testing idea is actually really good because Fatima's competitive. if I quiz her she'll want to get every answer right out of pride loool. and Seneca — I've heard of that actually. is it actually good for AQA sciences?

Seneca is genuinely excellent for AQA sciences — it's one of the most recommended GCSE revision tools and it's completely free. The way it works is it teaches a topic in small chunks, then immediately tests you on it, and spaces out the review based on what you got wrong. So it's doing active recall and spaced repetition automatically without her having to plan it.

For AQA specifically, the content is mapped to the exact specification so she's not wasting time on stuff that won't be examined. It covers biology, chemistry, physics, and maths among other subjects.

The competitive angle is perfect — Seneca actually has streaks and progress tracking so she can see herself improving. If she's the type who likes to complete things to 100%, it'll hook her. You could even set up a challenge: "Get all the biology modules to 100% by next weekend and I'll [incentive]." Sometimes a little external motivation helps at 16.
looool the bribery approach. that might actually work. I could take her out for food or something if she hits her targets. she's been wanting to go to this new dessert place in town

Bribery is an underrated educational tool — if it works, it works. A dessert trip for hitting her Seneca targets is perfectly reasonable motivation. You could even break it into milestones: biology to 80% = one trip, chemistry to 80% = another. Keeps her going rather than one big reward at the end that feels too far away.

Real talk though — you mentioned she wants medicine. If she's serious about that, it might also be worth looking into whether her school offers any med school preparation or whether there are summer programmes for aspiring medics. Some universities run free access programmes for students from underrepresented backgrounds. That kind of thing on her personal statement would be gold, and it shows her it's real and achievable.

oh that's a really good point. I'll look into that. she's at a state school and I don't think they push that kind of stuff to students like they do at private schools. someone needs to advocate for her and I guess that's me init

That's exactly it — students at state schools often have the ability but not the information. You stepping in to fill that gap is massive. A few things to look into:

- **IntoUniversity** — free academic support and mentoring, they have centres in Manchester
- **Medic Mentor** — free programmes specifically for aspiring medical students
- **The Sutton Trust** — runs summer schools at top universities for state school students, completely funded
- **Widening participation programmes** at Manchester, Leeds, Sheffield — they specifically recruit from nearby state schools

For now though, first things first: get her through these GCSEs with the grades she needs. Set her up on Seneca this weekend, do a quick quiz session to make it fun, and promise that dessert trip. August 21 will come fast.

it really will come fast tbh. I'm going to set her up on Seneca tonight and make a little revision timetable with her. and I'll look into those programmes you mentioned. wallah I hope she gets the grades because medicine would be everything for her and for our family

She's got a supportive older sister who's already navigated higher education and a professional career — that's a bigger advantage than she probably realises right now. Get the system in place, keep the encouragement steady, and let her do the work. You've got a month to make a real difference. Good luck to both of you.
thank you so much for all of this honestly. I feel better about it now. gonna go text Fatima the Seneca link before she goes to sleep. wish me luck getting her to actually use it loool
Good luck! Lead with the dessert incentive and she'll be on Seneca before midnight. You've got this, and so does Fatima.
